skill.name: plan-writing
skill.description: Structured task planning with clear breakdowns, dependencies, and verification criteria. Use when implementing features, refactoring, or any multi-step work.
skill.summary: Creates actionable task plans with verification criteria for features, refactoring, and multi-step development work.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 20 patterns (17 external_commands, 3 cryptographic) that are all FALSE POSITIVES. The SKILL.md file is documentation-only Markdown containing command examples in tables and code blocks, not executable code. Backticks are Markdown formatting, not shell execution. No cryptographic algorithms exist in the file. This is a safe planning skill that generates task breakdowns with verification criteria.

content.user_title: Create actionable task plans with verification criteria
content.value_statement: Complex development work often lacks clear structure and verification steps. This skill breaks down tasks into small, verifiable actions with explicit completion criteria.
actual_capabilities: Breaks complex tasks into 2-5 minute focused actions with single outcomes
Defines clear verification criteria for each task
Identifies task dependencies and critical paths
Adapts plan structure based on project type (new project, feature, bug fix)
Generates project-specific plan files saved to project root directory
Keeps plans concise with maximum 10 tasks per plan
limitations: Only works with read-only tools (Read, Glob, Grep) - cannot execute commands
Plans are documentation only - user must manually execute tasks
Requires user to provide project context for relevant recommendations
Not suitable for real-time collaboration or task tracking

use_cases.title: Feature Implementation Planning
use_cases.description: Break down new feature development into discrete, testable tasks with clear dependencies and verification steps for systematic implementation.

use_cases.title: Refactoring Project Organization
use_cases.description: Structure large-scale code refactoring into manageable phases with verification criteria to ensure system stability throughout the process.

use_cases.title: Bug Fix Documentation
use_cases.description: Create clear action plans for complex bug fixes that span multiple files or require understanding intricate system interactions.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Feature Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a task plan for adding user authentication to my Next.js application. Include setup, implementation, and verification steps.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: Simple feature addition with clear scope

prompt_templates.title: Refactoring Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to refactor our monolithic service into modular components. Create a phased plan that minimizes risk and allows incremental testing.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: Multi-file refactoring with dependency management

prompt_templates.title: Bug Investigation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Users report intermittent 500 errors in our API. Create a systematic investigation plan with hypothesis testing and rollback steps.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: Complex debugging with multiple potential causes

prompt_templates.title: Full Project Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a complete development plan for a new React + TypeScript project with testing, linting, and CI/CD configuration from scratch.
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert: Greenfield project with comprehensive tooling setup

output_examples.input: Create a task plan for adding user authentication to my Next.js application.
output: # User Authentication Implementation
## Goal
Add secure user authentication using NextAuth with email/password and OAuth providers.
## Tasks
- [ ] Install next-auth and bcrypt packages to Verify: npm install completes without errors
- [ ] Create /api/auth/[...nextauth].ts route to Verify: Route file exists with NextAuth configuration
- [ ] Add credentials provider to NextAuth config to Verify: Email/password login form submits successfully
- [ ] Implement session management to Verify: Protected routes redirect unauthenticated users
- [ ] Add OAuth providers (Google, GitHub) to Verify: OAuth login buttons appear and authenticate
## Done When
- [ ] Users can register, login, logout, and access protected routes with session persistence

output_examples.input: Plan a refactoring of our legacy jQuery codebase to React components.
output: # jQuery to React Migration Plan
## Goal
Incrementally migrate legacy jQuery components to React while maintaining functionality.
## Tasks
- [ ] Audit existing jQuery components and document behavior to Verify: Spreadsheet listing all components with dependencies
- [ ] Set up React build configuration alongside existing codebase to Verify: Both jQuery and React can coexist in same page
- [ ] Migrate isolated utility components first (buttons, modals) to Verify: Migrated components pass visual regression tests
- [ ] Create React wrapper for jQuery form handlers to Verify: Forms submit data correctly through React layer
- [ ] Replace page-level jQuery logic with React components to Verify: All user flows complete without JavaScript errors
## Done When
- [ ] All jQuery components replaced with React equivalents and jQuery dependency removed from package.json

best_practices: Start each plan with a single-sentence goal that clearly states what you are building or fixing.
Limit plans to maximum 10 tasks - if work exceeds this, split into multiple focused plans.
Always include specific verification criteria that anyone can follow to confirm task completion.
anti_patterns: Creating plans with 50+ micro-tasks that become overwhelming and lose focus on outcomes.
Writing generic tasks like 'Set up project' without specifying exact commands or expected outputs.
Copy-pasting template plans without adapting to the specific project context and requirements.

faq.question: What types of tasks are best suited for this planning approach?
faq.answer: Complex multi-step work like feature implementation, refactoring across multiple files, bug fixes requiring investigation, and new project setup. Simple one-off tasks do not need formal plans.

faq.question: How detailed should each task be in the plan?
faq.answer: Each task should represent 2-5 minutes of focused work with one clear outcome. Tasks should be specific enough that verification is unambiguous but not so granular they become micro-management.

faq.question: Can I modify the plan while working through tasks?
faq.answer: Yes, plans should evolve as you learn more during implementation. Update tasks as needed, mark completed items with [x], and add new tasks if you discover additional work.

faq.question: Where should plan files be saved in my project?
faq.answer: Save plan files in the project root directory with a descriptive name derived from the task (e.g., auth-feature.md, api-refactor.md). Avoid nesting plans in subdirectories.

faq.question: What if my task requires more than 10 subtasks?
faq.answer: Split the work into multiple related plans. For example, separate plans for 'Backend API Setup', 'Frontend Integration', and 'Testing and Validation' that together complete the full feature.

faq.question: How do I handle task dependencies in the plan?
faq.answer: List tasks in dependency order so prerequisite work comes first. You can also add inline notes like 'Requires: Task 2' to make dependencies explicit within the task description.
